speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Sandra Segal. Today is February 29, 1996. This is tape number three. Who do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have here my husband, Meyer Segal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi. Do you have anything you'd like to say to Meyer, anything you'd like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been married now for 37 years. And they've been good years. And I hope that we will have at least the next 37 better. If not the same, better.

speaker: interviewee
text: As happy and as healthy as the first 37.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. That's what I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything else that you'd like to add to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. That's about as good as we can hope for.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your wife's experiences have shaped your life, or how you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was a tremendous amount of a lack of self-confidence that may-- or probably stemmed from her experiences in the camps and fears that she had and her family had-- her brothers as well. And this was very noticeable. I think that it also led to a tremendous Zionistic fervor, a great support for not only Zionism in general, but moving to Israel. And her two brothers did succeed in moving to Israel. Unfortunately, we did not. But we do visit, and we do support Israel. And I think this is a good part of what may have shaped it. And I think that there's also a tremendous fear of a revival of the right-wing skinhead, Nazism, and this kind of thing. And I fervently hope that this will never happen. And I'm sure it won't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to add I don't know if-- who gets the see this, if it-- if it helps at all, that to get an idea across, that the soldiers, the liberators of the camps, all over the world, go speak to the public school children and tell them it is true. We were there. This is history. Face it. It happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As a teacher of history, I every once in a while run across students who will talk about the fact that it may not have happened. And it's interesting, because I teach mostly black students. And then we end up relating it to the experience with slavery. And it becomes something a little more real, and a little more palatable, or whatever you might want to say about it. But many of them, of course, they're supportive, and hope that this kind of thing never happens to anybody again. But there are those students who do hear the propaganda and do fall for the line that it's a Jewish plot, and this is Jewish, you know, revisionism of history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you teach the Holocaust in your history classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you teach it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's part of the American history course. I certainly incorporate it. I show usually the film And we have had survivors speak. Sunny has come in to speak a couple of times to classes at Redford. And that's how we usually handle it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is anyone in your family survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have a cousin come from Auschwitz after the war. He was the only survivor we knew of. And my parents brought him here. He lived with us for a number of years. And now he lives in New York and has a family of his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else either one of you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can think of at this point anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you for joining us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's mother. Her name was Dvora Raizel Stahl. Her maiden name was Junger, J-U-N-G-E-R. And she died in the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my mother's father. His name was Zvi-Hersch Stahl. And he also died in the camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my mother's father, my grandfather, the son of Zvi-Hersch and Dvora Raizel Stahl. His name was [INAUDIBLE] Stahl. He did-- he did survive the war, because he wasn't in it. He moved to New York before the war. This is my mother's mother. Her name was [INAUDIBLE] Stahl. Her Hungarian name was Yolan. And her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE]. And around her, she has to the upper left my mother. To the right is my mother's sister. She's still alive. She was hidden during the war. She lives in New York. Her name is Chaya Schmidt. And the two bottom ones, Shloimy and [INAUDIBLE], left and right, were the two boys that were taken away from the yeshiva in Belgium and gassed in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The young boy on the left, Shloimy, he was 23 years old. He was in the yeshiva. The whole yeshiva was taken to be gassed. He went to Mauthausen. And the one on the right, [INAUDIBLE], when he was taken out of the yeshiva, he was 21 years old. And he was sent to [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The man in the middle, we do not know. But the two on either end are my cousins, Shloimy and [INAUDIBLE] that were taken to the-- taken out of the school and sent to the gas chambers, as I showed you a younger version of them in the picture before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They happened to be friends of Elie Wiesel. Elie Wiesel knew our family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The older lady on the left is my mother's mother, [INAUDIBLE] Stahl. The lady on the right is my mother, Yanca Van Leeuwen, and later married Miller, in Toronto. And holding-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother's holding the baby, which is myself, Sandra Van Leeuwen Segal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These are my parents. My mother, Yanca Van Leeuwen Miller, and my father, Izak, Izak, Van Leeuwen. My father died when he was 38. This was taken before the war in 1937, two years before I was born. This was in [INAUDIBLE], Holland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my Aunt Rosie. And my Aunt Rosie was the one who the Nazi asked the husband, who should we take, you or your wife and kids? So he said, take my wife and kids. So she went and died in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy on the right is the son of my Aunt Rosie that I just described to you. He also went with his mother and four more siblings to be gassed in Auschwitz. The two on the left, Zahava Gealel, maiden name Stahl, lives now in Israel. And her brother, Avrahom Stahl, teaches at the Hebrew University, has written many books in sociology. He survived also. They both were in Bergen-Belsen with me. And their father, my mother's brother Wolf, died in Auschwitz at the age-- he went in at the age of 31. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to come over here? We can [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, there's more?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's sister, who is alive. She was hidden during the war. She moved to New York after the war. She is still living in New York. Her name is Chaya Fasten-Schmidt. They have a hyphenated name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the baby's name, but my mother said it was a cousin of ours. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The lady on the right is my mother, Yanca Van Leeuwen Miller. And her maiden name was Stahl. And that's, on her right, on our left, her sister, Chaya Fasten-Schmidt, living in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my mother's brother, Moshe Stahl, and his wife, Frieda. He asked us to be hidden during the war. And since we all couldn't go together, we opted to stay in [INAUDIBLE]. He was in hiding in Belgium. He took an assumed name, Marcel. And he became a diamond dealer after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's my mother's brother Wolf Stahl. He was 31 years old. He went into Auschwitz. He never made it out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Avrahom Stahl and his sister, Zehava Stahl. Her name in Holland was Lottie. And they are the children of Wolf Stahl, who died in Auschwitz. These are my first cousins. They now live in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The man on the left is my grandfather, [INAUDIBLE] Van Leeuwen. His name in Dutch was Levie. And the lady in front of him is his wife, Chaya-Sara Van Leeuwen. Her maiden name was Kohn. The lady on the right in back is my Aunt Dora, one of their daughters. She survived the war. She moved to Israel. And the young lady in front on the right is my Aunt Leentje, that nurse in Holland who was rolled up in the carpet when the Nazis came to look. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was one of my father's brothers who died in the concentration camp. I do not recall his name. That's my father's brother, Natan Van Leeuwen. He was 50 years old. He died in Auschwitz. That's my cousin, Chaya-Sara. Her last name is Levi. It used to be Van Leeuwen. She lives in Jerusalem, Israel. And her father was my father's brother, Wolf. And Wolf-- Wolf died in-- he died in Trobitz right as soon as we were released. These are five of my cousins. They all died in the concentration camp. I believe it was Auschwitz. And I think they were the children of my Aunt Lea Van Leeuwen, who was married to Izaak Pollack. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken before the war. The lady on the left is my Aunt Leentje, the nurse in Holland who was rolled up on the carpet who survived. And the little girl on the right, I think, was my father's sister, Cato. In English, that would be Kate Van Leeuwen. She was single. When she went into the war, she was aged 25. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is another picture of my Aunt Tante Leentje, my father's sister, Leentje Van Leeuwen, the one who was rolled up in the carpet and saved herself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Here, we have our first school photo after the war in first and second grade. My brother, Levy Van Leeuwen, who now lives in Kiryat Arba, Israel, and myself, Sandra Van Leeuwen Segal. Here we have a picture of my brother in the blue T-shirt, [INAUDIBLE] Van Leeuwen, who lives right outside of Tel Aviv in Israel in Hod HaSharon and his wife Edna. And the boy on the left, Guy. The young boy's name is Noah. And the girl on the right is Roni. This was on a trip to Niagara Falls. The lady on the left is myself, Sandra Segal, a fairly recent picture with my brother [INAUDIBLE] Van Leeuwen from Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of Rabbi Van Leeuwen, my brother, and his-- Levy Van Leeuwen and his wife, [INAUDIBLE]. They live Kiryat Arba, Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These are three of the younger children of my brother, Rabbi Levy Van Leeuwen in Kiryat Arba. The oldest is Ya'el. The middle one is Miriam, and the little one is Re'ut. These are the four oldest boys of my brother, Rabbi Van Leeuwen in Kiryat Arba. On the left, we have Hillel Van Leeuwen, then Izak Van Leeuwen, Yoni Van Leeuwen, and Mendi Van Leeuwen. This is my youngest son, Simcha Avraham Segal. He's an actuary for Metropolitan Life Insurance in New, York, Manhattan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my middle son, Mendi Segal. He's in Los Angeles in animation voiceover work. And he's the new singing voice of Bugs Bunny. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's the wedding shot of my oldest son, Yitzchak Zvi Segal to his new bride Linda. And they live in West Rogers Park in Chicago. He's a computer analyst. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These are my grandchildren. On the top, in the dark suits there on the right, is my 14-year-old Avraham Segal. To the left is Moshe Segal. He's 13. Down underneath is my granddaughter, Shifra. She's eight. Then her brother, Dovi. And then his brother, Yehuda. He's six. They all live in Chicago. And they're the children of Yitzchak Segal. That's myself, Sandra Segal and my husband, Meyer Segal. We were on a trip from Venice to Switzerland. This was taken on a train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is [INAUDIBLE] Sand Miller. He's originally from Czechoslovakia. He married my mother in 1954. He's alive. He still lives in Toronto. Here we have my parents, Izak and Yanca Van Leeuwen's marriage certificate. Although, this is not the original. The original was destroyed in the war. This is a copy that they made afterwards. This is a copy-- this is the original, not a copy, of my DP registration record of Sandra Van Leeuwen Segal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my medical clearance certificate, which was signed-- stamped by the Dutch Jewish community and the Red Cross, and also the Red [INAUDIBLE] stamp. This was my mother's passport when she took us over from Holland to Halifax, Canada. And that's a picture of my mother, Yanca Van Leeuwen, and myself, Sandra Van Leeuwen. These are the rest of the passport photos when we came from Holland to Canada. The boy on the left is my brother, Rabbi Van Leeuwen from Israel. And the little one on the right is my baby brother, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the information from the Dutch Red Cross, saying when we were taken, where we were taken, and when I was born, and where I lived at the time when we were taken. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a document showing that the Dutch community accepted me back in their community.
